title: Quadlet [Conatiner] ReloadCmd
body:
### Issue Description

I try to implement a `systemctl --user reload caddy` command. My caddy. container is:

```ini
[Unit]
Description=Caddy - The Ultimate Server with Automatic HTTPS
After=network-online.target

[Container]
Image=docker.io/library/caddy:2.10-alpine
ContainerName=caddy

Pod=caddy.pod

Volume=%h/.config/containers/systemd/caddy/conf:/etc/caddy:Z
Volume=caddy_data:/data:Z
Volume=caddy_config:/config:Z

# Reload command for [Service] ExecReload=...
# Note: '/usr/bin/podman exec caddy' is pre-pended automatically
ReloadCmd=sh -c "caddy reload --config /etc/caddy/Caddyfile --force"

[Service]
Restart=always

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
```

This generated to:

```bash
$ cat /run/user/1000/systemd/generator/caddy.service |grep ExecReload
# Reload command for [Service] ExecReload=...
ExecReload=/usr/bin/podman exec caddy sh -c "caddy\x20reload\x20--config\x20/etc/caddy/Caddyfile\x20--force"
```

but starting it gives:

```bash
$ systemctl --user reload caddy
Job for caddy.service failed.
See "systemctl --user status caddy.service" and "journalctl --user -xeu caddy.service" for details.
$ journalctl --user -xeu caddy.service | grep Error
Oct 15 18:32:03 coreos-test caddy[13931]: Error: reading config from file: open /etc/caddy/Caddyfile: no such file or directory
```

Note, caddy is running as expected. Did I miss omething?


### Steps to reproduce the issue

see above


### Describe the results you received

see above

### Describe the results you expected

That the service reloads its configuration, without kill -HUP etc., since it's builtin.

### podman info output

```yaml
$ podman version
Client:        Podman Engine
Version:       5.6.1
API Version:   5.6.1
Go Version:    go1.24.6
Git Commit:    1e2b2315150b2ffa0971596fb5da8cd83f3ce0e1
Built:         Thu Sep  4 02:00:00 2025
Build Origin:  Fedora Project
OS/Arch:       linux/amd64
$ cat /etc/os-release
NAME="Fedora Linux"
VERSION="42.20250914.3.0 (CoreOS)"
RELEASE_TYPE=stable
ID=fedora
VERSION_ID=42
VERSION_CODENAME=""
PLATFORM_ID="platform:f42"
PRETTY_NAME="Fedora CoreOS 42.20250914.3.0"
ANSI_COLOR="0;38;2;60;110;180"
LOGO=fedora-logo-icon
CPE_NAME="cpe:/o:fedoraproject:fedora:42"
HOME_URL="https://getfedora.org/coreos/"
DOCUMENTATION_URL="https://docs.fedoraproject.org/en-US/fedora-coreos/"
SUPPORT_URL="https://github.com/coreos/fedora-coreos-tracker/"
BUG_REPORT_URL="https://github.com/coreos/fedora-coreos-tracker/"
REDHAT_BUGZILLA_PRODUCT="Fedora"
REDHAT_BUGZILLA_PRODUCT_VERSION=42
REDHAT_SUPPORT_PRODUCT="Fedora"
REDHAT_SUPPORT_PRODUCT_VERSION=42
SUPPORT_END=2026-05-13
VARIANT="CoreOS"
VARIANT_ID=coreos
OSTREE_VERSION='42.20250914.3.0'
```

### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

Rootless

### Upstream Latest Release

Yes

### Additional environment details

Additional environment details

### Additional information

Additional information like issue happens only occasionally or issue happens with a particular architecture or on a particular setting

comments.body:
Does this command work if you run it manually outside of systemd:
```
/usr/bin/podman exec caddy sh -c "caddy reload --config /etc/caddy/Caddyfile --force"
```

Yes, from outside it's working:

```bash
core@coreos-test:~$ /usr/bin/podman exec caddy sh -c "caddy reload --config /etc/caddy/Caddyfile --force"
{"level":"info","ts":1760631347.8095074,"msg":"using config from file","file":"/etc/caddy/Caddyfile"}
{"level":"info","ts":1760631347.813785,"msg":"adapted config to JSON","adapter":"caddyfile"}
```

BTW, why is '/usr/bin/podman exec caddy' pre-pended automatically on ExecReload?

Thanks for the info. That's interesting because the two should basically behave the same way. I'll try to mimic this.

> BTW, why is '/usr/bin/podman exec caddy' pre-pended automatically on ExecReload?

This is what makes the command run inside the container. Otherwise, you'd be running it on the host machine

Thank you!

> > BTW, why is '/usr/bin/podman exec caddy' pre-pended automatically on ExecReload?
> 
> This is what makes the command run inside the container. Otherwise, you'd be running it on the host machine

How will this work? Inside the container, there is no Podman binary, nor Podman's socket. Maybe I misunderstood you? BTW, the command as such works from inside (shouldn't suprise):

```bash
core@coreos-test:~$ podman exec -ti caddy sh
/srv # caddy reload --config /etc/caddy/Caddyfile --force
2025/10/16 18:13:52.861 INFO    using config from file  {"file": "/etc/caddy/Caddyfile"}
2025/10/16 18:13:52.866 INFO    adapted config to JSON  {"adapter": "caddyfile"}
```

and also works from outside/host:

```bash
core@coreos-test:~$ /usr/bin/podman exec caddy sh -c 'caddy reload --config /etc/caddy/Caddyfile --force'
{"level":"info","ts":1760638742.2406247,"msg":"using config from file","file":"/etc/caddy/Caddyfile"}
{"level":"info","ts":1760638742.2450871,"msg":"adapted config to JSON","adapter":"caddyfile"}
```

I meant that this is what makes your command run inside the container. When you run `systemctl reload` it runs on the host machine. But, you want your actual command to run inside the container. So, you need to exec into it. Now, instead of having you add it manually, Quadlet does that for you. It's best to use Quadlet's capability since it also knows the name of the container.

As for your issue. Not sure why this does not work for you. It works for me (also with Podman 5.6.1)

I copied your `.container` file. You didn't provide the `caddy.pod` file so I just created a simple one:
```
[Pod]
``` 

I am able to start the service and reload it:
```
...
Oct 16 14:19:21 lima-default systemd[1004]: Reloading caddy.service - Caddy - The Ultimate Server with Automatic HTTPS...
Oct 16 14:19:21 lima-default podman[1814051]: 2025-10-16 14:19:21.911963523 -0400 EDT m=+0.027447204 container exec cbeb2f9c84979466916876f08e54ce8973b085bbc951dd558f054c6541485877 (image=do>
Oct 16 14:19:21 lima-default caddy[1814051]: {"level":"info","ts":1760638761.9299567,"msg":"using config from file","file":"/etc/caddy/Caddyfile"}
Oct 16 14:19:21 lima-default caddy[1814051]: {"level":"info","ts":1760638761.9302783,"msg":"adapted config to JSON","adapter":"caddyfile"}
...
```
Maybe check the permissions on the `Caddyfile`. I do not know why it would differ between the systemctl and the direct call, but could be?

Wow, no idea what's gone wrong last, today it's working! 

But I have noticed that when making changes to a (Windows) PC, `scp -r` to Linux CoreOS, start/stop/reload are not always consistent with the changes made — for whatever reason. However, I have not been able to reproduce this so far.

For the archive, here my pod

```ini
[Unit]
Description=Caddy Pod
After=network-online.target
Wants=network-online.target

[Pod]
PodName=caddy
HostName=caddy.localhost

# HTTP(s) ports
PublishPort=8080:80
PublishPort=8443:443/tcp
PublishPort=8443:443/udp

# admin port for caddy API
PublishPort=2019:2019

# Test port
PublishPort=8086:8086
```

and the starting of my struggle with Caddyfile (but, this is another storry)

```
{
	debug

	http_port 80
	https_port 443

	email me@example.com

	admin :2019 {
		# Only allow requests from localhost to prevent remote code execution
		origins localhost 192.168.1.13
		# enforce_origin
	}
}

:8086 {
	respond "Hello World"
}
```

Anyway, thanks - the issue can be closed. Shall I?

Thanks for confirming. I'm not sure this is the case. But, editing files on Windows and using them on Linux can cause issues due to line termination characters.
In any case, I'm closing this issue
